subject: Chemistry 11
grade_subject_fit: Chemistry 11 assessment/quiz proof using inline assessmentQuestion items already supported by schema.
unit_context: Students are learning to separate observations from inferences and use evidence to support claims about chemical reactions.
assignment_purpose: Define a proof for schema-level assessment and quiz output types with explicit teacher-only marking guide routes.
final_evidence_target: Students complete a short quiz and a more formal assessment that both require evidence-based reasoning.
student_task_flow: Use feedback or review to prepare for the assessment.
Complete the formal assessment using observations, evidence, and reasoning.
success_criteria: I can identify evidence of a chemical reaction.
I can separate observation from inference.
I can support a claim using specific evidence.

supports_scaffolds.label: Question variety
supports_scaffolds.support: Students answer multiple choice, short answer, and extended response questions.

supports_scaffolds.label: Response space
supports_scaffolds.support: Questions include n_lines so the renderer can size writing space appropriately.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Use short-answer response lines and direct evidence prompts.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Complete all questions with evidence and reasoning.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Use precise vocabulary and explain why alternative interpretations are weaker.

checkpoint_release_logic: Student assessment and quiz PDFs render separately from explicit teacher-only answer_key marking guide routes.
teacher_implementation_notes: Student assessment and quiz outputs must not show answer_key or marking_notes fields.
Teacher-only answer_key routes render marking guides from the same quiz and assessment source sections.
likely_misconceptions: Students may treat any visible change as proof of a chemical reaction.
Students may write an inference before recording the observation that supports it.
pacing_shape: Single-period proof fixture with student quiz, student assessment, and teacher-only marking guide routes.
assessment_focus: Evidence of chemical reactions, observation versus inference, and claim-evidence-reasoning.

quiz.title: Reaction Evidence Quiz
quiz.instructions: Answer each question using the best evidence from class and lab observations.
teacher_notes: Use as a low-stakes check before the formal assessment.

questions.q_text: Which observation is strongest evidence that a chemical reaction may have occurred?
choices: The solution is in a cup
questions.marking_notes: Gas formation can be evidence of a new substance forming when interpreted with context.

questions.q_text: Explain the difference between an observation and an inference.
questions.answer_key: An observation is what is directly noticed or measured. An inference is an interpretation or conclusion based on observations.
questions.marking_notes: Look for direct distinction between noticing/measuring and interpreting/concluding.

assessment.title: Chemical Reaction Evidence Assessment
assessment.instructions: Use complete sentences. Support your claim with specific observations and reasoning.
teacher_notes: Students may use their observation notes, but not the marking guide.

questions.q_text: A student mixes two clear liquids. The mixture becomes cloudy and a solid settles at the bottom. Make a claim about whether a chemical reaction may have occurred, then support it with evidence and reasoning.
questions.answer_key: A chemical reaction may have occurred because a precipitate formed. The cloudy mixture and solid settling suggest a new substance formed, though the claim should be tied to observations and lab context.
questions.marking_notes: Strong responses identify precipitate formation, cite both cloudiness/solid formation, and avoid claiming certainty without context.

questions.q_text: Label each statement as observation or inference: 1) The liquid turned blue. 2) A new substance formed. 3) The temperature increased by 5°C.
questions.marking_notes: Students should identify direct sensory/measured statements as observations and conclusion language as inference.

success_criteria: I support the claim with specific observations.
I explain how the evidence connects to the claim.
